title: TPU v1 · Standard-Cell Libraries: HVT for the MAC Sea, LVT for Critical Paths
oneLine: Synthesis builds from foundry-provided 28 nm cells rather than inventing circuits. Each AND, flop, or full adder comes in multiple drive strengths and threshold voltages: LVT is fast but leaky; HVT is slower but efficient. Constraints guide cell choice across TPU's 65K MACs—high-drive LVT on a few critical paths and HVT across noncritical logic to protect the 28–40 W power budget.

legend.name: Standard-cell library (Liberty/.lib)
legend.desc: The foundry's 28 nm building-block catalog contains characterized, fixed-layout cells for AND, NAND, MUX, flops, full adders, and more. Each entry supplies delay/slew tables, capacitance, leakage, dynamic power, and area across PVT corners. Synthesis can use only listed cells, so library quality caps QoR.

legend.name: Multiple drive strengths × multiple Vt options
legend.desc: One NAND2 may exist at X1/X2/X4/X8 drive strengths—larger drive is faster under load but costs area and power—and as LVT/SVT/HVT variants. LVT is faster with more leakage; HVT is slower with less leakage. Synthesis searches this speed/power/area product space.

legend.name: Synthesis: mapping + cell selection
legend.desc: During technology mapping and sizing, the tool chooses library cells that meet timing—about a 1.43 ns period for 700 MHz—while minimizing power and area. Static timing slack identifies critical versus relaxed logic and therefore the required drive and Vt.

legend.name: Result: critical paths versus noncritical logic
legend.desc: TPU separates naturally: a few cycle-limiting accumulation or decode paths use high-drive LVT cells, while the large, timing-relaxed 256×256 MAC array uses HVT wherever possible. Multi-Vt optimization is central to meeting a 28–40 W budget.

steps.cap: ① Inventory the library: each logic function has X1…X8 drive strengths and LVT/SVT/HVT variants, all characterized for delay, leakage, and area.
② Link all approved multi-Vt libraries and let synthesis choose cells under a 700 MHz (~1.43 ns) timing target and leakage objective.
③ On tight accumulation and control-decode paths, use high-drive LVT cells to close timing first.
④ Across the timing-relaxed but area-dominant 256×256 MAC array, replace cells with HVT; small per-cell leakage savings multiply across 65K units.
⑤ Produce a mixed-Vt netlist with a small fast critical subset and a large efficient baseline, meeting 700 MHz within the 28–40 W budget.

before: Collect complete libraries: Obtain 28 nm Liberty sets from the foundry across slow, typical, and fast process corners and target voltage/temperature, with LVT, SVT, and HVT variants. Synthesis and STA rely on these for delay, leakage, and area.
Set a Vt policy: Define permitted Vt classes and any cap on LVT usage because excessive LVT can exceed leakage budget. An array-heavy TPU should default toward HVT and reserve LVT for critical paths.

Constrain timing, slew, and capacitance: Specify the illustrative 1.43 ns/700 MHz clock plus set_max_transition and set_max_capacitance limits. These constraints determine the drive strength required at each node.
Set power intent: Provide leakage and dynamic-power targets so, after timing closes, synthesis actively swaps relaxed paths to HVT and downsizes cells rather than optimizing speed alone.
Control the allowed cell set: Apply set_dont_use to cells reserved for clock trees or considered risky, and set_dont_touch where structures must be preserved.
during: Core rule: synthesis can use only library cells. It performs technology mapping plus sizing/Vt selection, covering RTL Boolean logic with an available standard-cell network. Missing functions or drive options cannot be invented.
Drive follows load and slack. Higher fanout, longer interconnect estimates, and tighter slack push selection toward X4/X8; lightly loaded, relaxed paths use X1/X2 to save area and power.

Vt optimization dominates leakage. Close timing with sufficiently fast cells, then recover leakage by swapping all positive-slack paths to HVT and lower drive. At 256×256 scale, moving a large MAC population one Vt class yields substantial chip-level savings.

Reserve LVT for true critical paths. Because LVT is fast but leaky, use it only where the cycle cannot otherwise close. If LVT proliferates, revisit pipelining or architecture instead of forcing timing through leakage.
Optimize across corners. Delay is worst at slow corners, while leakage and hold may be worst at fast/hot corners. Cell choices must close setup, hold, and power across all required PVT corners.

State the source honestly: Standard-cell data comes from the foundry. Multi-Vt/multi-drive selection, SDC constraints, and leakage recovery are standard EDA practices. TPU structure, 700 MHz, 28 nm, and weight-stationary execution provide context; shown cell values are illustrative.

result.label: Input libraries (linked together)
result.value: 28 nm Liberty: LVT/SVT/HVT × X1…X8 drive × PVT corners

result.label: Mixed-Vt netlist
result.value: Critical → high-drive LVT; MAC sea → HVT for leakage

result.label: Reports
result.value: Area/timing/power QoR + cell counts by Vt class

after: Review Vt distribution and power: Check LVT/SVT/HVT counts and area. If LVT share or leakage is excessive, tighten policy or revise the architecture and resynthesize.
Review timing: Confirm setup slack ≥0 at 1.43 ns across corners and eliminate hold risk. Adjust drive/Vt or feed the path back for additional pipelining.
Run formal equivalence (LEC): Prove that the selected cell network implements the same function as RTL. Cell sizing and optimization must never alter behavior.
Iterate leakage recovery: Continue replacing positive-slack logic with HVT and lower drive while preserving timing until both timing and power converge.
Hand off to backend: Deliver the mixed-Vt netlist, SDC, and library set for place-and-route. Backend optimization will refine sizing and Vt using actual wire delays.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (foundry standard-cell data is not TPU-specific; multi-Vt/multi-drive selection and SDC/power constraints are standard EDA synthesis practices, and exact synthesis figures are not public.)
